title: Lesson 265
transcript: So let's refocus on what we are talking about here in domain number one. Each of the modules that we are talking and going through in domain number one. are really the components of what we call a security program. So they are the really important ways that the organization addresses its security management requirements. Again, this entire domain is focused on security management. How do we manage the security requirements of the organization itself? In this module, we're going to talk about probably the most important thing, and that is any security program needs to be built on a foundation of policies that reflect on goals and objectives of the organization itself. If there was one really important module in this entire course, this would be it because it really provides the foundation for effective security to exist in any organization. In fact, what we refer to this as is the perfect model of security. It will work in any organization, but it has to be structured in what you're gonna see here. And you'll also see that there are many failure points that exist. in how companies try and really get to this model into this structure. But every piece that you're going to see here is really important and needs to be implemented perfectly for security to have not only the support but also the effectiveness and the best way to implement security is really going to be represented here. So let's start talking about this. And the first thing that we need to understand is what a policy is. I know we've mentioned that word many times, but policies are really corporate laws, just like any other government relies on laws to be able to tell people what is, what is not acceptable. In a corporate environment, policies will dictate and communicate management's intentions as it relates to something that is obviously very important. In other words, reflects on goals and objectives of the organization itself. And because security directly contributes to those goals and objectives. It has to be based on a foundation of well-written, well-communicated, well-implemented, well-understood, well-reminded policies that reflect on goals and objectives. So let's take a look at what this structure actually looks like. And so the first thing that you need in any organization is what we refer to as a overarching security policy and note the word policy singular here what this means is that this is what sets the tone and creates the culture in the organization for effective security to actually exist and be possible This is a statement that must come from the CEO and no one else. This is where companies fail. Typically, all companies will have a policy that says security is everything. is important and is everyone's responsibility. But typically that policy is something that the security function goes around and dictates to people. If you do that, you're going to fail. This message has to come from the CEO. It's the CEO that has to say security is very important in our organization. we believe in it, we understand its importance because it directly contributes to our goals and objectives that are always focused on increasing value of this organization and therefore Security is everyone's responsibility, but we, upper management, the executive, the board of directors, really believe in it and understand its importance. We will support it. we will direct it, we will drive it, but it is everyone's responsibility. Imagine what that statement would do to set the tone and create the culture in the organization. And what's important with this as well is that it cannot be something that is communicated once by the CEO. It needs to be constantly re-communicated on a regular basis. The more often the better. But not only does it need to be re-communicated on a regular basis, it has to be demonstrated by those that are at the top. the CEO and others that are directly accountable for achieving the goals of the organization which needs security to focus on those things. This is something that is the most important and in my years of experience I've seen this is what makes security functions and security programs fail, but it is the most important thing that sets the tone and creates the culture. What we have after this, and note the arrows going downwards because this is a top down model for security security today does not work bottom up any longer that structure does not work in fact it will fail for sure it has to be a top down you need this first which sets the tone creates the culture and then what we need are what we call functional policies and some people will call them functional implementing policies and you'll see why in a few minutes here but note the word policies plural. This means that you are going to have a whole bunch of policies that reflect on goals and objectives of the organization and it's not uncommon for companies to have many, many policies. They could have a policy that deals with data classification. They could have a policy that deals with remote access. They may have a policy that deals with anti-malware protection. They may have a policy that talks about asset management and so on. It's pretty common for companies to have a whole bunch of these. Now, if you know what the word policies means, which we said is a corporate law that dictates what is, what is not acceptable. A policy simply communicates management's intentions as it relates to something that is very important. The policy itself does not really provide security. It simply communicates something that we all have to understand at all levels of the organization itself. So let's use an example here. Let's say in our organization we have a policy that says every workstation that's connected to the network has to have malware protection on it. Now that is a typical policy that you would find in most organizations. It simply tells everyone that you cannot connect anything to the network unless it has malware protection on it. But does that policy actually protect the network against malware? The answer to that is obviously no. What we need to do is have these, each of the policies that exist at this level need to be implemented. And so the way that you implement each of the policies that you see at this level is through what we're going to put down here and of course I will apologize ahead of time for not being able to draw really well but that's why we've got slides as part of your materials that will look much better than what I can ever draw but what we're gonna have down here and note that the arrows are always going down and so here we're gonna have what are called standards. And here we've got procedures. And here we've got baselines. And here we've got guidelines. And so when you look at these four elements, these four structures, it is how we implement each of the policies that exist at this level. So if our company had 100 policies, that dictate what's important for everybody to understand at this level. Each one of them would require the implementation of really security controls that are really a combination of standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines. Now we need to understand what the difference of these are. Standards are defined as specific hardware and software solutions. That's really what the word standard used in this context actually means. I know we use that word a lot in security but in this context it actually means the specific solutions that we have standardized on to implement that policy which at this level says every workstation that's connected to the network has to have malware protection on it so the company may have standardized on Norton or McAfee or something like that. That would be the specific hardware and software solution that we have chosen. Now why did we choose that standard? Probably because in our organization it is the most efficient cost-effective solution to implement that policy that says every workstation that's connected to the network has to have malware protection on it. Now procedures, you know what procedures are. Procedures are simply step by step required mandatory actions. An example of a procedure that we would need to implement in order to address that same policy that we're using here as an example Might be here are the steps that you have to go through in order to install McAfee on your workstation or laptop before you can connect it to the network. Or maybe a better example might be here are the steps that you have to go through in order to update your virus signature files that your copy of McAfee on your laptop uses to scan from the latest signature files now those are step-by-step required actions we may automate those in fact most companies do if you rely on people to do procedures properly there's always going to be the A chance of errors and mistakes that get made. So as much as possible when it comes to procedures, we want to automate those. So maybe when you log in in the morning, there's a script that runs as part of your log on process. that goes out, fetches the latest signature files that are stored on a server somewhere across the network and brought down to your machine so that your copy of McAfee can use the latest signature files. Even though we've automated those steps, they are still procedures. The word baseline means a minimum level of security. And so we would establish baselines to also support this implementation of this policy. And so that could be related to configuration parameters. Here are the minimum levels of configurations that you need to establish for all of your Windows laptops. Whereas other technologies that might also be connected to the network could have different configuration parameters. Each of those would be reflected and known as baselines, minimum levels that must always be met. Anything below that line is not good enough, anything above that line would be good enough, but we call those baselines. and guidelines as the word implies means just suggestions recommendations not anything that is mandatory but you should or you should not like you could say all right you should not plug in any untrusted usb device into your laptop when it's connected to the network It simply says you shouldn't do something, so they are guidelines. But the bottom line is, when you put these things together, standards, procedures, baselines, and guidelines, It really is the security controls that you and I are typically involved in implementing in the organization. And certain policies, depending on what they are and what they address, may require several standards, several procedures, several baselines, and so on, to implement each of the policies so each of the policies at this level would require the controls but look and don't miss this point look how we got to the security controls in a top-down fashion with support commitment conviction and involvement and direction from those that are accountable like the CEO and the board of directors implemented through a foundation of well-written, well-communicated, well-understood policies that people know and provides that foundation for effective security to exist and implemented through the actual security controls that are standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines. Now this looks pretty good, but we're not finished yet because there are some key points that are missing from this. We've talked earlier about how anything that security does, really needs to be aligned with goals and objectives. In fact, we talked about how security governance, which means what security does, should always be aligned with corporate governance. Here's how you achieve that goal. We need the establishment of a corporate security governance committee that is going to be made up of key people that will represent key areas of the organization, that are accountable for achieving the goals and objectives of the organization, they are the ones that are going to drive what security does and they're going to prioritize and justify each of the initiatives and activities that the security function is going to undertake. In other words, we're going to align what is important to the organization with what security does by the establishment of these people that are directly accountable with what the organization has to achieve through corporate governance and so if we were to write this into the diagram here it would probably go right here and we would call this a corporate security governance committee and it's important to call this what it actually is you cannot call it a advisory committee you cannot call it a steering committee because that's what that committee would do simply steer or advise. This is much more important than that. These people that make up this committee are the ones that are actually accountable, that's the key word, accountable for all of those things that equate to value to the organization, like compliance. meeting privacy requirements, protecting assets, protecting people and so on. That's why this committee is very important. In fact, here's where companies fail. Usually security understands the importance of this committee, but they are the ones that go around and ask people from key areas of the organization to be involved. If you do that, you've already failed. This cannot be established by the security department because if you do that, you're doing a bottom-up approach to security. this needs to be established by those that are accountable in fact it is the CEO that needs to go around and involve people in this committee the CEO needs to go around and say you need to be on this committee and you need to be in this committee and you need to be in this committee in fact The way that this reporting structure exists is that that committee will have to report ideally to the board of directors and failing that, they would have to report to the person that is actually appointed by the board to oversee what the organization does. And because security today directly contributes to goals and objectives, this committee has to report to the CEO. That's the only way that it can work. And so I guess we can go both ways here. But in fact, this committee should be chaired by the CEO so that the people that are actually in here understand how important it is in what they provide in structuring and dictating and prioritizing what security ultimately does. You see, it's not the security function that tries to justify what they need to do for the organization. It's a top-down approach that is based on people that actually are in here and understand how important it is to have securities direct involvement in things that they are directly accountable for contributing to goals and objectives and value. And there's one more piece here that I think makes this entire model perfect. And that is the initial thing that we talked about here, which is that communication that comes from the CEO that sets the tone and creates the culture for perfect security to exist. This needs to be communicated by, ideally, the board of directors. But failing that, again, it would have to be the CEO that on a regular basis not only communicates the importance of security and how it's everyone's responsibility, but actually demonstrates that. Imagine what this would look like in an organization where we have this support, commitment, conviction that is top down for security to do the things that it needs to. So that when security does come around, if this is the structure that exists, You are not seen as someone that gets in the way and imposes on the business. But instead, security in this fashion enables the business. And in fact, when security comes around, everyone will view you, part of security, as helping and contributing towards goals and objectives, which is ultimately what we always want. Now here is the problem. when you look at this it's the perfect model that we want to achieve but there are failure points in every single part of this perfect model We can fail here. In fact, that's what I've seen in most of my experience. We don't have that culture, that tone that is set by the CEO and the board of directors. We can fail here because we don't have well-written, well-communicated and understood policies that everybody at all levels understand. We choose the wrong standards, procedures, baselines and guidelines that are possibly not cost-effective and so on. We can fail here as well Maybe we don't even have this committee. And even if we do, it's not called a governance committee that reports to the CEO. In fact, it's just made of people that simply advise and don't care what they do as part of that job. And of course, we can fail up here as well because they are the ones that ultimately provide this structure that makes it work. This is the perfect model. Something that you should take a mental note of because you can see what drives effective, efficient security. It's always support and commitment from the top but it's always going to be based on policies that dictate what's important to the organization. And if you take this structure and apply it not only to the exam questions that you may see to be able to pass the CISSP exam, but you can apply this model and try and achieve it in everything that you do from the for the rest of your career in your security lives you'll be a better more valuable security professional if you try and explain things in this perspective now the last number of slides that you have in this section here we'll talk about each of these components that we've talked about so for example here are policies and what's important about policies is that they really provide the foundation for all of this to work properly but policies have to be well written well communicated well understood, well reminded on a regular basis for them to be effective. The first excuse people give you when they break policy is, I didn't even know there was a policy, or I remember seeing that policy five years ago, or that policy is too long. Very difficult to understand. And here are what we call some of the other components. Implementing policies are usually security policies that reflect on specific valuable assets and goals of the organization. And again as we said before there could be a whole bunch of them. Those are some of the examples that you see on your screen. And then the definitions of standards, specific hardware and software solutions that we've chosen because they are the most effective and efficient. way for the organization to achieve its goals and objectives. Procedures, step-by-step required actions, and those are some examples. Anytime you see a list just like this one, it's not meant to be an all-inclusive list. we're just giving you examples of what in this case procedures may look like in order to implement policies. Here are your baselines which are as we said before minimum levels of security and Guidelines which provide simply suggestions, recommendations. They are not cast in stone. Perfect model of security.
